texto: Anthropologist Gary Weaver suggested looking at culture as a kind of iceberg: one portion is clearly visible above the surface of the water, while the much larger chunk of ice is hidden below. The part above the water can be considered surface culture––what we can physically see or hear, including behavior, words, customs, language, and traditions. Underneath the water, invisible to all, is the deep culture. This place includes our beliefs, values, assumptions, worldview, and thought processes.
explicacao: Citação de David C. Pollock sobre temas ligados a reflexao. “Anthropologist Gary Weaver suggested looking at culture as a kind of iceberg: one portion is clea…” articula reflexao, culture, identity em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como identity.

frase_original: Anthropologist Gary Weaver suggested looking at culture as a kind of iceberg: one portion is clearly visible above the surface of the water, while the much larger chunk of ice is hidden below. The part above the water can be considered surface culture––what we can physically see or hear, including behavior, words, customs, language, and traditions. Underneath the water, invisible to all, is the deep culture. This place includes our beliefs, values, assumptions, worldview, and thought processes.

texto: When we think of the word culture, obvious representations such as how to dress, eat, speak, and act like those around us come to mind. But learning culture is more than learning conformity to external patterns of behavior. Culture is also a system of shared concepts, beliefs, and values. It is the framework from which we interpret and make sense of life and the world around us.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a David C. Pollock: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de sentido da existência e experiência vivida. O trecho “When we think of the word culture, obvious representations such as how to dress, eat, speak, and …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, culture, identity pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de trabalho e identity.

frase_original: When we think of the word culture, obvious representations such as how to dress, eat, speak, and act like those around us come to mind. But learning culture is more than learning conformity to external patterns of behavior. Culture is also a system of shared concepts, beliefs, and values. It is the framework from which we interpret and make sense of life and the world around us.

texto: Caregivers, like all of us, inevitably reflect their culture's attitude toward children and life. The story goes that when Pearl Buck was a child in China, someone asked how she compared her mother to her Chinese amah. Buck replied, "If I want to have a story read, I go to my mother. But if I fall down and need to be comforted, I go to my amah." Her mother's culture valued teaching and learning, while her amah's placed a greater value on nurture. Even as a child, Buck instinctively knew the difference.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a David C. Pollock: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de sentido da existência e experiência vivida. O trecho “Caregivers, like all of us, inevitably reflect their culture's attitude toward children and life.…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que educacao, caregivers, culture pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de culture e third-culture-kids.
